insight: Shalev's Domination Rule (SDR) states that having a unit on the board should be at least as good as not having it — i.e., it is never strictly worse to possess a unit than to lack it. SDR is violated when a mechanic punishes a player for *having* something (units, health, higher lifespan), but not when it punishes them for *buying* something.
author: apooche
context: Community discussion about AoE attack mechanics and whether they break SDR, with apooche clarifying the precise definition.

insight: AoE attack (hitting all enemy units simultaneously) breaks SDR because it makes it strictly worse to have bought units that would die to it — the opponent would have been better off not purchasing those units at all. By contrast, 'Destroy all enemy units' does not break SDR because having the units was not worse than not having them before the destruction.
author: apooche
context: Discussion about whether AoE damage effects violate SDR, with apooche and http404error distinguishing punishing purchases from punishing possession.

insight: An effect that grants the caster gold per enemy unit on the board (e.g., 'gain a gold per enemy unit') breaks SDR, because it makes it strictly worse for the opponent to have purchased those units — they would have been better off not having them.
author: apooche
context: apooche giving concrete examples of SDR-breaking vs. non-SDR-breaking effects during the AoE design discussion.

insight: Absorbers (blocking units that soak damage) fundamentally determine the pace of a game. Large absorbers can warp the game toward high-economy formats and can render certain attackers like Centurion much weaker by denying them the opportunity to deal meaningful damage.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about hypothetical crystallizer-type units and the systemic role of absorbers in balancing attacker viability.

insight: Counters to large absorbers include freeze-based units (Iso Kronus, Scorchilla), Tatsu Nullifier, and Vai Mauronax. Apollo and Deadeye Operative are also noted as 'attackers' that bypass blocking entirely, providing alternative pressure against absorb-heavy strategies.
author: consensus
context: Community brainstorming about counters to big absorbers beyond freeze mechanics.

insight: The value of Green energy (tech resource) is commonly estimated at 4/3 gold but is context-dependent and often lower, especially in early turns like p2's DDC opening where the Conduit's Green cannot efficiently be converted to Drones instead. In practice, Green may be worth closer to 1 gold equivalent in some early-game positions.
author: consensus
context: spiritfryer and mtanzer discussing how to value the Green generated by an early Conduit in p2's DDC opening when calculating tech cost.

insight: In p2's dd ddc opening, the 4 gold left over on turn 2 is generally worth less than 4 gold at a later point in the game because it cannot be spent very efficiently at that stage — approximately 2 goes to an Engineer and 2 floats toward the next turn's purchase.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer explaining the inefficiency of p2's leftover gold in the DDC opening to spiritfryer.

insight: Players commonly describe opening sequences using keyboard-hotkey shorthand: 'D' for Drone, 'E' for Engineer, 'C' for Conduit, etc., allowing compact notation like 'dd ddc' or 'dd dde ddd' for the first several turns. This shorthand is well-understood for openings but breaks down in mid/late game where replays or screenshots are preferred.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about whether a formalized Prismata notation system is feasible, referencing existing informal shorthand already used by the community.

insight: Units in Prismata historically had internal code names distinct from their display names (e.g., Tarsier was internally called 'Tesla Tower'). A community bot pulling unit names from the wrong database field would display these internal names rather than display names.
author: apooche
context: apooche explaining why a Discord bot was showing playtest/internal names for units instead of their proper display names.

insight: Galvani Drone is considered a risky purchase because it can be exploited by the opponent — the frontline health is a liability. A proposed 'Static Drone' design (Drone+Galvani hybrid without frontline) was judged worse than simply buying a Drone then floating gold into a Galvani Drone later, because the Drone generates income during the float turns and Galvani's click opportunities accumulate.
author: apooche
context: apooche analyzing a fan-designed Static Drone unit by comparing it turn-by-turn to the buy-drone-then-buy-Galvani line.

insight: mtanzer and another player (punf) conducted a spreadsheet simulation to find the optimal drone count for wall-based absorb strategies. The model assumed: build only economy for a fixed number of turns, then switch entirely to attack, and always build the minimum soak to achieve absorb. The approach was exploratory rather than yielding a definitive formula.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer describing a prior simulation project to spiritfryer who was asking about theorycrafting optimal drone counts for absorb strategies.

insight: Deadeye Operative interacts interestingly with drone-count-parity mechanics: because the player clicks Deadeye after the opponent has finalized their drone count for the turn, the Deadeye user can control which tech color the opponent receives by choosing whether or not to click and kill a drone.
author: mqp
context: mqp analyzing a fan-designed Parity Filter unit that gives different tech colors based on even/odd drone count, noting Deadeye's timing creates interesting control interactions.
